enrichment.short_description: ElevenLabs is an AI audio research company that provides realistic text-to-speech, voice cloning, dubbing, and conversational voice agents through a web app and developer API.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from $5/month billed annually, $6/month billed monthly)
enrichment.company_size: 500-1,000+ employees (estimates vary by source; roughly 70 employees in 2023 growing to several hundred by 2025-2026)
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States (additional offices in London, UK and Warsaw, Poland)
enrichment.seo_title: ElevenLabs Review 2026: AI Voice Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ElevenLabs in 2026: pricing tiers from Free to Enterprise, voice cloning, text-to-speech, dubbing, and conversational AI agents reviewed in depth.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
ElevenLabs is an artificial intelligence audio technology company best known for producing some of the most natural-sounding synthetic speech available on the market. Founded in 2022 by Piotr Dąbkowski, a former Google machine learning engineer, and Mati Staniszewski, a former Palantir deployment strategist, the company was started after the two Polish-born founders noticed how poor the dubbing was on foreign-language films and set out to build AI models that could generate expressive, human-like voices in any language. ElevenLabs is headquartered in New York City, with additional engineering and operations hubs in London and Warsaw. The company has grown extremely quickly: it raised a Series C in early 2025 and, by February 2026, closed a $500 million Series D that valued the business at roughly $11 billion, more than tripling its valuation within a year. Investors have included Andreessen Horowitz, ICONIQ Growth, and Sequoia, among others, and total disclosed funding sits in the hundreds of millions of dollars.

At its core, ElevenLabs sells access to proprietary deep-learning voice models through a browser-based Speech Studio and a REST API. The flagship capability is text-to-speech, where users type or paste text and select from a large library of stock AI voices or a cloned voice to generate natural, emotionally inflected audio in dozens of languages. The platform's Voice Cloning tools range from Instant Voice Cloning, which needs only a short audio sample, to Professional Voice Cloning, which trains a higher-fidelity model from a longer, cleaner recording. Beyond narration, ElevenLabs offers Speech-to-Text transcription, an AI Dubbing Studio that can translate and re-voice video content while preserving the original speaker's tone, a Sound Effects generator, a music generation tool, and Conversational AI, a framework for building low-latency voice agents that can be plugged into phone systems, customer support workflows, and applications via API and native SDKs.

ElevenLabs prices its products on a monthly credit system, where credits are consumed based on characters generated, minutes transcribed, or agent usage. The Free plan includes 10,000 credits per month with access to text-to-speech, speech-to-text, sound effects, voice design, and a limited number of Studio projects, but output is for non-commercial use only. The Starter plan is $6/month (or roughly $5/month billed annually) and unlocks instant voice cloning, a commercial license, and 30,000 monthly credits. Creator, at $22/month, adds professional voice cloning and 121,000 credits, and is the tier most solo creators, podcasters, and YouTubers land on. Pro costs $99/month for 600,000 credits and higher-fidelity 44.1kHz PCM audio via the API. Scale, at $299/month, adds team seats and multiple professional voice clones for 1.8 million credits, and Business, at $990/month, scales further to 6 million credits with low-latency options and up to ten workspace seats. An Enterprise tier offers custom credit volumes, dedicated support, HIPAA-ready Business Associate Agreements, custom SSO, and elevated concurrency for large media, gaming, and customer-service deployments.

ElevenLabs' primary users span several very different audiences: independent content creators and podcasters who need affordable narration and dubbing; publishers, audiobook producers, and news organizations localizing content into multiple languages; game studios and film/TV post-production teams needing character voices and ADR; and, increasingly, enterprise software teams building voice agents for call centers, IVR replacement, and in-app assistants through the Conversational AI product and API. Because the API is metered and developer-friendly, ElevenLabs has also become a common backend voice provider embedded inside other SaaS products, from video editors to customer support platforms.

Competitors in the AI voice space include Google Cloud Text-to-Speech and Amazon Polly for basic synthetic speech, Play.ht and Murf AI for creator-focused narration, Resemble AI and Descript's Overdub for voice cloning, and OpenAI's own audio models for conversational use cases. ElevenLabs differentiates primarily on perceived voice realism and expressiveness — independent listening tests and creator communities have repeatedly ranked its voices among the most natural-sounding available — as well as breadth: few competitors combine text-to-speech, voice cloning, dubbing, sound effects, music, and conversational agents in a single platform with one credit pool. Notable facts include ElevenLabs' rapid rise to a multibillion-dollar valuation in under three years, its role in several high-profile AI-generated-audio controversies that pushed the company to introduce voice-verification and misuse-detection safeguards, and its widely used free-tier API that many indie developers rely on for prototyping voice features before scaling to paid usage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ElevenLabs builds AI models that generate lifelike speech, clone voices, translate and dub video, and power real-time conversational voice agents. Founded in 2022 by Piotr Dąbkowski and Mati Staniszewski, the company is headquartered in New York City with additional teams in London and Warsaw.

The platform is accessed through a web-based Speech Studio for creators and a developer API for teams embedding voice into their own products, with usage across both metered on a shared monthly credit system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include multilingual text-to-speech with a large stock voice library, instant and professional voice cloning, speech-to-text transcription, an AI dubbing studio for localizing video, a sound-effects generator, AI music generation, and a Conversational AI framework for building voice agents.
Developers can integrate any of these capabilities through a REST API and official SDKs, making ElevenLabs a common voice backend inside third-party apps, games, and customer support tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ElevenLabs uses a freemium, credit-based pricing model with seven tiers: Free, Starter ($6/month), Creator ($22/month), Pro ($99/month), Scale ($299/month), Business ($990/month), and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier.

Each tier raises the monthly credit allowance, unlocks higher-fidelity audio and more workspace seats, and adds commercial usage rights; annual billing on any paid plan works out to roughly two months free compared with paying monthly.

features.title: Multilingual text-to-speech
features.description: Generate natural, emotionally expressive speech from text in dozens of languages using a large library of stock AI voices.

features.title: Instant and professional voice cloning
features.description: Clone a voice from a short sample for quick use, or train a higher-fidelity professional clone from a longer recording.

features.title: AI dubbing studio
features.description: Automatically translate and re-voice video or audio content into another language while preserving the original speaker's tone and timing.

features.title: Speech-to-text transcription
features.description: Convert spoken audio into accurate text transcripts, useful for captioning and content repurposing.

features.title: Conversational AI agents
features.description: Build low-latency voice agents for phone systems, customer support, and in-app assistants using API and SDK tooling.

features.title: Sound effects and music generation
features.description: Generate short sound effects and music tracks from text prompts for use in video, games, and podcasts.

features.title: Developer API and SDKs
features.description: Access every ElevenLabs capability programmatically to embed voice generation directly into third-party products.

features.title: Commercial licensing
features.description: Paid tiers from Starter upward include a commercial license covering generated audio, voice clones, and dubbed content.

pros: Widely regarded as producing some of the most natural, human-like AI voices available
Combines text-to-speech, cloning, dubbing, and conversational agents in one platform and one credit pool
Generous free tier and low-cost Starter plan make it accessible to individual creators
Robust developer API makes it easy to embed voice generation into other products
cons: Credit consumption can escalate quickly for high-volume commercial use, making costs hard to predict
Professional voice cloning and team seats are locked behind mid-tier and higher plans
Enterprise pricing is not publicly disclosed and requires a sales conversation
Voice cloning technology has raised misuse and deepfake concerns industry-wide, prompting added verification steps

faqs.question: Is ElevenLabs free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, ElevenLabs offers a Free plan with 10,000 monthly credits, but generated audio on the Free plan is limited to non-commercial use. Commercial rights start on the Starter plan at $6/month.

faqs.question: What languages does ElevenLabs support?
faqs.answer: ElevenLabs' text-to-speech and dubbing models support dozens of languages, and its voices can speak most major world languages with natural pronunciation.

faqs.question: Can I clone my own voice with ElevenLabs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Instant Voice Cloning is available from the Starter plan using a short audio sample, while Professional Voice Cloning, available from the Creator plan, produces a higher-fidelity clone from a longer recording.

faqs.question: Does ElevenLabs have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, ElevenLabs provides a REST API and official SDKs so developers can integrate text-to-speech, voice cloning, dubbing, and conversational agents into their own applications.

faqs.question: How does ElevenLabs pricing work?
faqs.answer: ElevenLabs uses a monthly credit system. Higher-priced plans, from Starter through Business, include more credits, higher audio quality, additional workspace seats, and more voice clones, with a custom Enterprise tier for large-scale needs.

faqs.question: Who founded ElevenLabs and when?
faqs.answer: ElevenLabs was founded in 2022 by Piotr Dąbkowski and Mati Staniszewski, and is headquartered in New York City with offices in London and Warsaw.

use_cases.title: Audiobook and podcast narration
use_cases.description: Creators and publishers use ElevenLabs to generate natural-sounding narration and convert written content into audio at a fraction of the cost of studio voice talent.

use_cases.title: Video localization and dubbing
use_cases.description: Media teams use the AI Dubbing Studio to translate and re-voice video content into multiple languages while preserving the original speaker's tone.
use_cases.audience: Media companies and video production teams

use_cases.title: Voice agents for customer support
use_cases.description: Enterprises build low-latency conversational voice agents on ElevenLabs' API to handle phone-based customer service and in-app voice assistants.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise product and customer support teams

features: Text to speech, speech to text, sound effects

enrichment.short_description: Eleventy (11ty) is a free, open-source JavaScript static site generator designed to be simpler and more flexible than alternatives like Jekyll, supporting many template languages out of the box.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Eleventy (11ty) explained: the free, open-source JavaScript static site generator that supports 10+ template languages and zero-config builds.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Eleventy, commonly abbreviated 11ty, is a free and open-source static site generator built in JavaScript. It was created in 2017 by developer Zach Leatherman, who was inspired by Ruby-based Jekyll and wanted an equally simple but more flexible tool built natively for the JavaScript ecosystem. The name is a personal touch: Leatherman has explained that 'Eleventy' comes from a childhood mispronunciation of the number eleven that his grandmother liked to retell as a story, giving the otherwise technical project a distinctly playful identity. Unlike many modern JavaScript frameworks, Eleventy was deliberately designed around minimal configuration, fast build times, and zero required client-side JavaScript, which made it a favorite among developers who wanted fast, simple, content-focused websites without adopting a full framework like Next.js or Gatsby.

Technically, Eleventy works by reading a directory of source files, most commonly written in Markdown, Nunjucks, Liquid, Handlebars, Pug, EJS, HAML, or plain HTML, and compiling them into static HTML files that can be hosted anywhere, including free static hosts, CDNs, or traditional web servers. A defining feature is that it supports more than ten templating languages simultaneously in the same project, and, critically, it does not force any particular front-end framework or build tooling on the developer. Data can be layered in through JSON, JavaScript, or front-matter files, and Eleventy's data cascade system lets developers compose global, directory-level, and per-page data cleanly. Because Eleventy ships no client-side JavaScript by default, sites built with it tend to score very well on page-speed and Core Web Vitals metrics, which has made it a popular choice for content sites, documentation, blogs, and marketing pages where SEO performance matters.

Eleventy is entirely free and open source, distributed under the MIT license, with its source code hosted on GitHub. There is no paid tier, subscription, or commercial edition of the core tool; anyone can install it via npm and use it for personal or commercial projects at no cost. In September 2024, the project entered a new chapter of its history when Font Awesome, the icon and web-tooling company, acquired the project's stewardship, and Zach Leatherman joined the Font Awesome team to continue leading its development while keeping the tool open source. In October 2024, Eleventy released version 3.0, its first major version to natively support ECMAScript modules, followed by continued performance-focused releases such as version 3.1, described by the maintainers as measurably faster and smaller than prior releases.

In March 2026, Font Awesome introduced a rebrand of the project under the name 'Build Awesome,' positioning it as an all-in-one site-building companion to Font Awesome and Web Awesome products. The rebrand proved controversial within the developer community, and shortly afterward Font Awesome paused both an associated crowdfunding campaign and the renaming plans following concerns that promotional emails to the community had not reached their intended recipients as expected. As of mid-2026, the project continues to be actively maintained, with the 11ty name still very much in active use across its documentation, plugin ecosystem, and community resources, even as its long-term branding direction remains an open conversation.

Eleventy's primary users are web developers, technical writers, and small teams building blogs, documentation sites, marketing pages, and portfolios who want full control over their HTML output without the overhead of a JavaScript framework. It is frequently used in the Jamstack ecosystem alongside static hosts like Netlify and Vercel. Its closest competitors include Jekyll (Ruby-based, one of the original static site generators), Hugo (Go-based, known for extremely fast builds), Astro and Next.js (which output static or hybrid sites but center around component-based frameworks), and Gatsby. Eleventy differentiates itself through its unopinionated, framework-agnostic approach, its support for many templating languages in a single project, and its historically minimal learning curve compared to component-framework-based generators. Because it produces plain HTML with no required JavaScript runtime, it remains one of the most SEO- and performance-friendly static site generators available, and it continues to be maintained as a genuinely free, community- and Font Awesome-backed open-source project rather than a venture-funded commercial product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Eleventy (11ty) is a free, open-source static site generator written in JavaScript, created in 2017 by Zach Leatherman as a simpler, more flexible alternative to Jekyll.

It compiles Markdown, Nunjucks, Liquid, and other template formats into fast, dependency-free static HTML, and has been stewarded by Font Awesome since 2024 with Leatherman continuing as lead maintainer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Eleventy supports more than ten templating languages in a single project, requires zero client-side JavaScript by default, and offers a flexible data cascade for composing page, directory, and global data.
Its plugin ecosystem covers image optimization, RSS feeds, pagination, and internationalization, and its output is plain static HTML that can be deployed to any static host or CDN.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Eleventy is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or usage limits for the core software.
Costs, if any, come only from optional third-party hosting or CI services used to build and deploy the resulting static site, not from Eleventy itself.

features.title: Multi-template-language support
features.description: Write pages in Markdown, Nunjucks, Liquid, Handlebars, Pug, EJS, HAML, or plain HTML, all within the same project.

features.title: Zero required client-side JavaScript
features.description: Eleventy outputs plain static HTML by default, keeping sites fast and lightweight without forcing a JavaScript runtime on visitors.

features.title: Data cascade
features.description: Compose page, directory-level, and global data through JSON, JavaScript, or front-matter files for flexible content modeling.

features.title: Fast incremental builds
features.description: Eleventy rebuilds only what has changed during development, keeping local preview loops fast even on larger sites.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Official and community plugins add image optimization, RSS feeds, pagination, syntax highlighting, and internationalization support.

features.title: Framework-agnostic output
features.description: Eleventy does not require adopting React, Vue, or any other UI framework, giving developers full control over HTML output.

features.title: Deploy anywhere
features.description: Since the output is static HTML, sites can be hosted on any static host, CDN, or traditional web server.

features.title: Active open-source development
features.description: Maintained under Font Awesome stewardship since 2024, with regular releases such as version 3.0 and 3.1 improving speed and module support.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or usage limits
Produces fast, lightweight static HTML with no required client-side JavaScript
Supports many templating languages in the same project, easing migration from other generators
Widely used and well-documented within the Jamstack and JavaScript communities
cons: Requires comfort with the command line and JavaScript tooling, unlike no-code website builders
Smaller plugin and theme marketplace compared to larger ecosystems like WordPress
Recent Font Awesome rebranding efforts ('Build Awesome') created community uncertainty about the project's direction
No built-in visual editor, so content authors need to work directly with source files or a headless CMS

faqs.question: Is Eleventy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Eleventy is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid plans or usage restrictions.

faqs.question: Who created Eleventy?
faqs.answer: Eleventy was created in 2017 by developer Zach Leatherman, and has been stewarded by Font Awesome since September 2024.

faqs.question: What templating languages does Eleventy support?
faqs.answer: Eleventy supports more than ten template languages, including Markdown, Nunjucks, Liquid, Handlebars, Pug, EJS, HAML, and plain HTML, often mixed within the same project.

faqs.question: Does Eleventy require React or another JavaScript framework?
faqs.answer: No. Eleventy is framework-agnostic and outputs plain static HTML with no required client-side JavaScript, though it can integrate with front-end frameworks if desired.

faqs.question: Is Eleventy the same as 'Build Awesome'?
faqs.answer: In March 2026, Font Awesome introduced a rebrand called Build Awesome for the project, but the rebranding and an associated fundraiser were paused shortly after, and the 11ty name remains in active use as of mid-2026.

faqs.question: What is Eleventy commonly used for?
faqs.answer: Eleventy is commonly used to build blogs, documentation sites, marketing pages, and portfolios where fast load times and SEO performance are priorities.

use_cases.title: Developer blogs and documentation
use_cases.description: Developers use Eleventy to build fast, SEO-friendly blogs and technical documentation sites without adopting a heavier JavaScript framework.

use_cases.title: Jamstack marketing sites
use_cases.description: Agencies and small teams use Eleventy to build performance-focused marketing and landing pages deployed to static hosts like Netlify.

use_cases.title: Static portfolios and personal sites
use_cases.description: Individuals use Eleventy's simple templating system to build personal portfolios and resume sites hosted for free on static hosting platforms.

features: Full source code under MIT license
No usage restrictions or paid tiers

enrichment.short_description: Elgg is a free, open-source social networking engine written in PHP that lets organizations build their own private online communities with blogging, groups, and file sharing.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted; hosting and services cost extra)
enrichment.seo_title: Elgg Review 2026: Open-Source Social Networking Engine
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Elgg in 2026: the free, open-source PHP social networking engine for building private communities, with features, hosting needs, and alternatives explained.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Elgg is a free, open-source social networking engine that gives organizations the building blocks to create their own online community platforms, complete with blogging, microblogging, file sharing, groups, and user networking features. It was created in 2004 by Ben Werdmuller and Dave Tosh, who had been developing informal ideas about applying social-networking concepts to online education over the preceding year. Werdmuller had experience building and moderating online communities since 1995, while Tosh was a postgraduate researcher in online education, and together they set out to build software that would let institutions run Facebook-like community features on their own infrastructure rather than relying on commercial social networks. The pair founded a company, Curverider Ltd, to continue developing Elgg and to offer related professional services. Elgg is frequently credited as one of the first platforms to bring mainstream social-networking concepts into educational and enterprise software, well ahead of most commercial social platforms doing the same.

The project went through several ownership changes over its history. In April 2009, co-founder Ben Werdmuller stepped away from the project, and development leadership passed to Brett Profitt. In December 2010, Curverider was acquired by Thematic Networks, and stewardship of the Elgg codebase was subsequently transferred to a dedicated non-profit body, the Elgg Foundation, which continues to govern, protect, and promote the project today. This move to non-profit governance was intended to keep Elgg independent of any single commercial owner and to preserve its open-source character for the long term.

Technically, Elgg is built on PHP and MySQL, using its own templating and plugin architecture rather than adopting an off-the-shelf web framework. The current generation of the software requires PHP 8.1 or higher, roughly 256MB of PHP memory, and a MySQL 8 database, and it relies on a working cron scheduler to send digests and notifications. Elgg's core installation provides fundamental community features out of the box, including user profiles, blogging and microblogging (activity streams), private and public groups, file and media sharing, and granular access-control settings that let community owners decide exactly who can see or interact with each piece of content. Additional functionality, from custom themes to specialized community tools, is added through a plugin system; because there is no single official plugin marketplace, most third-party plugins are distributed through the community repository or sold independently by individual developers, and plugin compatibility with the current core version can be inconsistent.

Elgg is dual-licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL) and the MIT License, meaning the core software is free to download, modify, and deploy commercially with no licensing fee. There is no official paid Elgg product; costs come entirely from self-hosting infrastructure, which can start from a few dollars a month on shared hosting for small communities under roughly 500 active users, and from any custom development or support services an organization chooses to commission from the Elgg community or freelance developers. Because Elgg is self-hosted, organizations retain full control of their community's data, which has made it especially attractive to institutions with data-sovereignty or privacy requirements.

Elgg's core users have historically included universities and research institutions building private academic or alumni networks, non-profits and social-cause organizations running community and advocacy platforms, and enterprises building internal, Facebook-like intranets for staff collaboration. Documented deployments include use by Oxfam, community platforms tied to the Spanish 15-M anti-austerity movement, and installations at academic institutions including Harvard and Stanford. Competing approaches include BuddyPress (a WordPress plugin that adds similar social features to a WordPress install), commercial community platforms like Mighty Networks and Discourse (forum-focused), and enterprise intranet tools like Jive or Simple Machines Forum for narrower use cases. Elgg differentiates itself by being a dedicated, purpose-built social networking engine rather than a plugin bolted onto a CMS, giving it deeper native support for profiles, groups, and access control, at the cost of a smaller plugin ecosystem and a steeper self-hosting and maintenance burden than hosted alternatives. As of 2026, Elgg remains actively maintained by the Elgg Foundation and its open-source contributor community, with releases continuing to track modern PHP versions, even as the platform occupies a more niche position than it did during the height of social-networking-software interest in the late 2000s and early 2010s.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Elgg is a free, open-source social networking engine created in 2004 by Ben Werdmuller and Dave Tosh, giving organizations the tools to build their own private community platforms rather than relying on commercial social networks.
Now governed by the non-profit Elgg Foundation, it is built on PHP and MySQL and dual-licensed under the GPL and MIT licenses, making it free to self-host for commercial or non-commercial use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Elgg's core provides user profiles, blogging and activity streams, public and private groups, file sharing, and granular access controls out of the box.
Additional functionality is added through a plugin architecture, and communities are extended through custom themes and third-party or community-built plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Elgg itself is completely free and open source with no licensing fee, but organizations must budget for self-hosting infrastructure, which can start from a few dollars a month on shared hosting for small communities.
There is no official paid tier; larger deployments typically pay for higher-capacity hosting and, optionally, custom development or support services from the community.

features.title: User profiles and networking
features.description: Members create profiles and connect with one another, forming the social graph at the center of an Elgg-powered community.

features.title: Blogging and activity streams
features.description: Built-in blogging and microblogging tools let members post updates that flow into a community-wide or group-level activity stream.

features.title: Groups
features.description: Public and private groups allow communities to organize around shared interests, courses, or projects.

features.title: File and media sharing
features.description: Members can upload and share files and media within their profiles or groups, with access controlled per item.

features.title: Granular access control
features.description: Every piece of content can be restricted to specific audiences, from fully public to a single group or individual.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Core functionality can be extended through community-built and third-party plugins covering themes, integrations, and specialized tools.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Organizations install Elgg on their own PHP/MySQL infrastructure, retaining full control over data and customization.

features.title: Non-profit stewardship
features.description: The Elgg Foundation governs ongoing development, keeping the project independent of any single commercial owner.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Full data ownership and control since it is entirely self-hosted
Purpose-built for social networking, with deeper native profile, group, and access-control features than a bolted-on CMS plugin
Long track record dating back to 2004, with proven use by universities and non-profits
cons: Requires self-hosting expertise and ongoing PHP/MySQL server maintenance
Smaller and less consistent plugin ecosystem than larger platforms like WordPress
No official marketplace, so premium plugins are scattered across independent developer sites
Smaller, more niche community today than during the platform's peak in the late 2000s and early 2010s

faqs.question: Is Elgg free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Elgg is free and open source under a dual GPL/MIT license. There is no software licensing fee; costs come only from self-hosting infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who created Elgg and when?
faqs.answer: Elgg was created in 2004 by Ben Werdmuller and Dave Tosh, and is now governed by the non-profit Elgg Foundation.

faqs.question: What are the server requirements for Elgg?
faqs.answer: The current version of Elgg requires PHP 8.1 or higher, roughly 256MB of PHP memory, a MySQL 8 database, and a working cron scheduler for notifications and digests.

faqs.question: Can Elgg be used commercially?
faqs.answer: Yes. Elgg's dual GPL/MIT license permits commercial use, modification, and deployment at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Who typically uses Elgg?
faqs.answer: Universities, non-profits, and organizations building private community platforms or internal social intranets are Elgg's most common users.

faqs.question: How is Elgg different from BuddyPress?
faqs.answer: Elgg is a standalone, purpose-built social networking engine, while BuddyPress is a plugin that adds social features on top of an existing WordPress installation.

use_cases.title: University and alumni networks
use_cases.description: Universities use Elgg to build private academic communities and alumni networks with profiles, groups, and discussion features.

use_cases.title: Non-profit and advocacy communities
use_cases.description: Non-profits and social causes use Elgg to run member communities and coordinate advocacy without relying on commercial social networks.

use_cases.title: Internal enterprise social intranets
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy Elgg internally as a private, self-hosted social network for staff collaboration and knowledge sharing.

features: Full self-hosted source code under GPL/MIT license
User profiles, groups, blogging, and file sharing

enrichment.short_description: Ellie is a daily planner app that combines a brain-dump task list with a drag-and-drop Kanban-style day timeline, built by solo developer Chris Raroque for iOS, macOS, Windows, and the web.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ellie Planner reviewed: the brain-dump and drag-and-drop daily planner app for iOS, macOS, Windows and web, with pricing and feature breakdown for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Ellie Planner, usually referred to simply as Ellie, is a personal daily-planning application built around two core ideas: a free-form 'brain dump' area for capturing every task and thought without friction, and a visual, drag-and-drop day timeline for turning that unstructured list into a realistic schedule. The app was built by Chris Raroque, a Dallas, Texas-based solo indie developer who has also worked on other software projects, including an earlier app called Mogul and a venture-focused startup called Aloa. Ellie began as a personal side project to solve Raroque's own struggle with traditional to-do list apps, which tend to let unfinished tasks pile up indefinitely without ever forcing a realistic look at how much time is actually available in a day. According to Raroque's own account of building the product, Ellie grew organically to several hundred daily active users while being run and supported largely by a single developer, a detail that has made it a frequently cited example in indie-maker and solo-SaaS-builder communities.

Functionally, Ellie works around a simple daily loop. Users start by dumping every task, idea, or to-do into an unstructured 'braindump' list, without needing to assign times or priorities upfront. From there, tasks are dragged onto a visual timeline or Kanban-style day board, effectively timeboxing each item into a specific block of the day. This design is meant to combat the common failure mode of long, static to-do lists, where tasks accumulate without ever being scheduled into real time. Ellie also supports recurring tasks, subtasks, due dates, unlimited labels for organizing tasks by project or context, and basic time tracking with lightweight analytics on how time is actually spent across a day or week. To fit into an existing digital calendar workflow, Ellie integrates with Google Calendar and Apple Calendar, and it supports Siri Shortcuts for quick task capture on Apple devices.

Ellie is available as a native iOS app for iPhone and iPad, a native macOS app, a Windows application, and a web app, giving it broader desktop coverage than many competing planner apps; notably, there is no native Android app, so Android users are limited to the web version. This platform spread, particularly the inclusion of dedicated macOS and Windows apps alongside iOS, differentiates Ellie from many mobile-first productivity apps that treat desktop as an afterthought.

On pricing, Ellie follows a freemium model. The Free plan allows unlimited task creation and access to the core braindump workflow across the iOS and web apps at no cost and without requiring a credit card. The paid tier, Ellie Pro, unlocks the full timeboxing/timebox mode, both calendar integrations, unlimited labels and subtasks, recurring tasks, and due dates. Ellie Pro is offered as a $9.99 per month subscription, or as a one-time lifetime purchase for a higher upfront price rather than a recurring monthly fee, giving users who dislike subscriptions a way to pay once. The company also offers a 50% discount off the yearly price for students who verify their status by contacting support directly.

Ellie's target users are individuals who have struggled with conventional to-do list apps and want a lighter-weight, more visual way to plan a single day at a time, rather than a heavyweight project-management tool built for teams. It appeals particularly to knowledge workers, freelancers, students, and anyone practicing timeboxing or time-blocking as a personal productivity method. Its closest competitors include Todoist and TickTick for general task management, Sunsama and Motion for calendar-integrated daily planning, and Structured for its similarly visual timeline-based approach to daily scheduling. Ellie differentiates itself with its explicit two-step brain-dump-then-schedule workflow, its native multi-platform desktop support beyond just mobile, and its origin and continued operation as a lean, largely solo-built product rather than a venture-backed team, which has kept its feature set focused and its pricing comparatively simple.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ellie Planner is a daily planning app built around a brain-dump task list and a drag-and-drop day timeline, created by Dallas-based solo developer Chris Raroque.
It is available on iOS, macOS, Windows, and the web, with a free tier for basic task capture and a paid Pro tier that unlocks timeboxing and calendar integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ellie's workflow starts with an unstructured braindump area for capturing tasks, then lets users drag items onto a visual day timeline to timebox their schedule.
Additional features include recurring tasks, subtasks, due dates, unlimited labels, basic time tracking and analytics, and integrations with Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, and Siri Shortcuts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ellie offers a free plan with unlimited task creation and core braindump functionality with no credit card required.

Ellie Pro adds timebox mode, calendar integrations, and unlimited labels and subtasks for $9.99/month, with a one-time lifetime purchase option also available and a 50% student discount on annual pricing.

Native apps for iOS, macOS, and Windows in addition to the web, unusual for a planner app of its size
One-time lifetime purchase option available for users who prefer not to subscribe
Simple, focused two-step brain-dump-then-schedule workflow that is easy to learn

Built and supported largely by a single developer, which can affect the pace of updates and support
Core timeboxing feature and calendar integrations are locked behind the paid Pro tier

faqs.question: Is Ellie Planner free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ellie offers a free plan with unlimited task creation and braindump functionality. The Pro tier, which unlocks timebox mode and calendar integrations, costs $9.99/month or is available as a one-time lifetime purchase.

faqs.question: Does Ellie have an Android app?
faqs.answer: No. Ellie is available on iOS, macOS, Windows, and the web, but there is currently no native Android app; Android users can use the web version.

faqs.question: Who built Ellie Planner?
faqs.answer: Ellie was built by Chris Raroque, a solo indie developer based in Dallas, Texas, who has also worked on other projects including Mogul and Aloa.

faqs.question: Does Ellie integrate with Google Calendar?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ellie Pro integrates with both Google Calendar and Apple Calendar so scheduled tasks appear alongside existing calendar events.

faqs.question: Is there a student discount for Ellie Pro?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ellie offers a 50% discount off the yearly Pro price for verified students who contact support directly.

faqs.question: What makes Ellie different from a regular to-do list app?
faqs.answer: Ellie separates task capture from scheduling: tasks first go into an unstructured braindump list, then get dragged onto a visual day timeline to timebox them into real time, rather than sitting on a static, ever-growing list.

use_cases.title: Daily timeboxing for knowledge workers

enrichment.short_description: EmailOctopus is a London-based email marketing platform offering affordable newsletter campaigns, automation, and landing pages, built as a low-cost alternative to larger email service providers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: EmailOctopus in 2026: pricing plans, automation features, and landing pages reviewed for this budget-friendly email marketing platform from London.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
EmailOctopus is an email marketing platform founded in 2014 in London, England, built specifically to offer a lower-cost alternative to larger email service providers like Mailchimp. The company has grown steadily as a lean, self-sustaining business rather than a venture-backed hypergrowth startup, and reports indicate it is run by a small team of roughly ten to fifteen people while serving a large customer base — over 50,000 customers who collectively send more than 19 billion emails a year through the platform. That combination of a small internal team and a very large customer base is a recurring talking point in coverage of the company, and it reflects a deliberate focus on keeping the product simple, self-serve, and inexpensive to operate rather than expanding into a large enterprise sales organization.

The platform's core function is standard email marketing: users import or grow a subscriber list, design campaigns using a drag-and-drop or HTML editor, and send newsletters, promotions, or automated sequences to segmented audiences. EmailOctopus layers in marketing automation for sequences like welcome series and drip campaigns, basic audience segmentation, subscriber list management with import/export tools, and landing page and signup form builders that let users grow a list without needing a separate website. Under the hood, EmailOctopus has historically used Amazon Simple Email Service (Amazon SES) as its sending infrastructure, which is part of how it has been able to keep its own pricing significantly lower than competitors that operate and price around their own proprietary sending infrastructure.

EmailOctopus prices itself around subscriber-count tiers rather than email-volume tiers, following the model most of the email marketing industry uses. The Free plan supports up to 2,500 subscribers and 10,000 emails sent per month, with EmailOctopus branding included on sent campaigns, statistics retained for only 30 days, one landing page and one signup form, and access limited to a single user. The paid Pro plan starts at $9 per month (billed annually; noted as roughly $8/month in some slightly older pricing snapshots) for a base tier of 500 subscribers with unlimited monthly sends, and the price automatically scales upward as a subscriber list grows, following EmailOctopus's stated 'AutoUpgrade' approach to billing. Pro unlocks full control over email design and branding removal, indefinite statistics retention, unlimited landing pages and signup forms, and support for multiple user accounts on one workspace. For very large senders, EmailOctopus offers custom Enterprise pricing for accounts exceeding roughly 500,000 subscribers. The company also extends a 20% lifetime discount on any paid plan to verified non-profit organizations.

EmailOctopus's target users are small businesses, solo creators, bloggers, and startups that are price-sensitive and want core email marketing functionality — campaigns, automation, segmentation, and landing pages — without paying for the more elaborate CRM, e-commerce, or multichannel features bundled into higher-priced platforms. Its most direct competitors are Mailchimp, the dominant incumbent in small-business email marketing; MailerLite, another budget-focused competitor with a similar audience; ConvertKit, which targets creators and course sellers specifically; and Brevo (formerly Sendinblue), which bundles email with SMS and CRM tools. EmailOctopus differentiates itself almost entirely on price, positioning its paid plans as 40 to 60 percent cheaper than mainstream alternatives at comparable subscriber counts, while intentionally keeping its feature set focused rather than trying to match the breadth of larger all-in-one marketing suites. This price-first positioning, combined with its small team and reliance on Amazon SES infrastructure, has let EmailOctopus sustain a profitable, self-funded business model in a market segment usually dominated by heavily funded competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: EmailOctopus is a London-based email marketing platform founded in 2014, positioned as a lower-cost alternative to larger providers like Mailchimp.
The company is run by a small team of roughly ten to fifteen people, serving more than 50,000 customers who send over 19 billion emails a year through the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes campaign creation, marketing automation sequences, subscriber list management, audience segmentation, and landing page and signup form builders.
EmailOctopus has historically relied on Amazon SES for sending infrastructure, which helps it maintain significantly lower pricing than competitors using proprietary sending infrastructure.

paragraphs: EmailOctopus prices around subscriber count: the Free plan covers up to 2,500 subscribers and 10,000 monthly emails, while the Pro plan starts at $9/month for 500 subscribers with unlimited sends and automatically scales as a list grows.
Non-profits receive a 20% lifetime discount on paid plans, and custom Enterprise pricing is available for accounts exceeding roughly 500,000 subscribers.

Simple, focused feature set that avoids the complexity of larger all-in-one marketing suites

Fewer advanced CRM, e-commerce, and multichannel features than larger platforms like Brevo or HubSpot
Small internal team may mean slower feature development compared to venture-backed competitors
Landing pages and forms are limited to one each on the free plan

faqs.question: Is EmailOctopus free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, EmailOctopus offers a free plan covering up to 2,500 subscribers and 10,000 emails per month, with EmailOctopus branding included on campaigns.

faqs.question: How much does EmailOctopus Pro cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan starts at $9 per month for 500 subscribers with unlimited monthly sends, and the price automatically scales up as your subscriber list grows.

faqs.question: When was EmailOctopus founded?
faqs.answer: EmailOctopus was founded in 2014 and is headquartered in London, England.

faqs.question: Does EmailOctopus offer a discount for non-profits?
faqs.answer: Yes, verified non-profit organizations receive a 20% lifetime discount on any paid EmailOctopus plan.

faqs.question: What email sending infrastructure does EmailOctopus use?
faqs.answer: EmailOctopus has historically built its sending infrastructure on Amazon Simple Email Service (Amazon SES), which helps it offer lower prices than competitors running their own proprietary infrastructure.

faqs.question: How is EmailOctopus different from Mailchimp?
faqs.answer: EmailOctopus offers a narrower, more focused feature set than Mailchimp but prices its paid plans roughly 40 to 60 percent cheaper at comparable subscriber counts, appealing to price-sensitive small businesses and creators.

enrichment.short_description: E-MailRelay is a free, open-source, lightweight SMTP proxy and store-and-forward mail transfer agent written in C++ for Linux and Windows, often used as a local spam-filtering relay.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: E-MailRelay explained: the free, open-source C++ SMTP proxy and store-and-forward mail server for Linux and Windows, with features and setup guidance.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
E-MailRelay, referred to on gappsy as EmailRelay, is a free and open-source SMTP proxy and store-and-forward mail transfer agent (MTA) written in C++. It is distributed and versioned through SourceForge, where its releases for both Linux and Windows are hosted, including a graphical installation wizard for Windows users who prefer not to configure the software from the command line. Unlike large, general-purpose mail servers such as Postfix or Exim, E-MailRelay is intentionally narrow in scope: it is built to sit between a mail source and a destination mail server, receiving, storing, and forwarding email messages, rather than trying to be a full-featured mailbox or webmail platform.

Architecturally, E-MailRelay runs as a single, lightweight process using the same non-blocking input/output model employed by high-performance software like Squid and nginx, which gives it strong scalability and a small resource footprint even on modest hardware. It performs three related jobs: acting as an SMTP storage daemon that accepts and stores incoming email, acting as an SMTP forwarding agent that relays stored messages onward to another server, and acting as a POP3 server that lets local email client software retrieve the stored messages. Forwarding is typically configured to a fixed 'smarthost,' a designated upstream mail server responsible for final delivery, though E-MailRelay can also be configured to perform its own routing logic when needed. A notable capability is its support for passing every message through a user-defined external program before it is forwarded; this hook lets administrators plug in spam filters such as SpamAssassin, or write custom scripts that can drop, re-address, or otherwise edit messages as they pass through the relay. It also supports DNSBL-based connection blocking to reject connections from known spam sources at the network level.

Because of this design, E-MailRelay is most commonly deployed in a handful of specific scenarios: as a personal or small-office internet mail server that filters spam before final delivery, as a local development or testing SMTP server that captures outbound mail from applications without actually delivering it externally, and as a lightweight relay/proxy sitting in front of a heavier production mail server to add filtering or store-and-forward resilience. Its C++ codebase and minimal dependencies make it straightforward to compile and run on constrained systems, including older hardware or embedded Linux environments, where a full mail server stack like Postfix would be comparatively heavyweight.

E-MailRelay is entirely free and open source, and there is no commercial edition, paid tier, or licensing fee associated with the software; users download source or pre-built binaries directly from its SourceForge project page at no cost. As is common for smaller, long-running open-source infrastructure projects, there is no dedicated company behind it, no disclosed founding date beyond its long-standing presence on SourceForge, and no formal support contracts — support comes from project documentation and community channels rather than a commercial vendor.

E-MailRelay's target users are systems administrators, developers, and hobbyists who need a small, dependency-light SMTP relay or proxy rather than a full-featured mail server platform. It is frequently compared with Postfix, Exim, and sendmail, all of which are far more feature-complete general-purpose MTAs capable of running full production mail infrastructure, and with lighter developer-focused tools like MailHog or smtp4dev, which are built specifically for capturing outbound test email during software development. E-MailRelay's differentiation is its narrow, single-purpose design: it does one job — storing, filtering, and forwarding SMTP mail — extremely efficiently, with a small codebase, low resource usage, and straightforward configuration, rather than trying to compete with full mail server suites on breadth of features. This makes it a durable, low-maintenance choice for administrators who specifically need a relay or proxy layer rather than a complete mailbox and delivery system.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: E-MailRelay (EmailRelay) is a free, open-source SMTP proxy and store-and-forward mail transfer agent written in C++, distributed through SourceForge for Linux and Windows.
It is designed as a narrow, single-purpose relay rather than a full mail server platform, focusing on receiving, storing, filtering, and forwarding SMTP mail.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: E-MailRelay runs as a single non-blocking process, acting simultaneously as an SMTP storage daemon, an SMTP forwarding agent, and a POP3 server for local mail clients.
It supports piping messages through external filtering programs such as SpamAssassin, DNSBL-based connection blocking, and configurable routing to a fixed smarthost or custom destinations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: E-MailRelay is completely free and open source, with no paid tier, licensing fee, or commercial edition.
It is distributed as source code and pre-built binaries through its SourceForge project page at no cost.

features.title: SMTP forwarding agent

features.title: POP3 server
features.description: Serves stored messages to local email client software using the POP3 protocol.

features.title: External filter hooks
features.description: Pipes messages through a user-defined program, allowing integration with spam filters like SpamAssassin or custom editing scripts.

features.title: DNSBL connection blocking
features.description: Rejects connections from known spam sources using DNS-based blocklists.

features.title: Non-blocking I/O architecture
features.description: Runs as a single lightweight process using the same event-driven model as Squid and nginx for efficient resource usage.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on both Linux and Windows, with a graphical installation wizard available for Windows deployments.

features.title: Lightweight, dependency-light codebase
features.description: Written in C++ with minimal dependencies, making it easy to compile and run on constrained or older hardware.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Very lightweight and resource-efficient compared to full mail server suites
Flexible filtering hook lets administrators plug in SpamAssassin or custom scripts
Runs on both Linux and Windows with a graphical installer for Windows users
cons: Narrower feature set than full mail server platforms like Postfix or Exim
No official commercial support, so troubleshooting relies on documentation and community resources
Configuration is command-line and config-file driven, which requires technical comfort
Smaller, less active community compared to mainstream MTA projects

faqs.question: Is EmailRelay (E-MailRelay) free?
faqs.answer: Yes, E-MailRelay is completely free and open source, distributed through SourceForge with no licensing fee or paid tier.

faqs.question: What platforms does E-MailRelay support?
faqs.answer: E-MailRelay runs on both Linux and Windows, and includes a graphical installation wizard for Windows deployments.

faqs.question: What does E-MailRelay actually do?
faqs.answer: It acts as an SMTP proxy and store-and-forward mail transfer agent, receiving, storing, optionally filtering, and forwarding email to another mail server, while also serving stored mail to local clients over POP3.

faqs.question: Can E-MailRelay filter spam?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can pipe incoming messages through an external program such as SpamAssassin, and supports DNSBL-based blocking to reject connections from known spam sources.

faqs.question: How is E-MailRelay different from Postfix?
faqs.answer: E-MailRelay is a narrower, lightweight SMTP proxy and relay rather than a full-featured production mail server like Postfix; it focuses specifically on storing, filtering, and forwarding mail rather than complete mailbox and delivery infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who maintains E-MailRelay?
faqs.answer: E-MailRelay is maintained as an open-source project hosted on SourceForge, without a dedicated commercial company behind it.

use_cases.description: Developers run E-MailRelay locally to capture outbound application email during testing without delivering it externally.

use_cases.title: Lightweight relay in front of production mail servers
use_cases.description: Administrators deploy E-MailRelay as a store-and-forward proxy layer in front of a heavier mail server to add filtering and resilience.

SMTP storage, forwarding, and POP3 serving

enrichment.short_description: Emby is a personal media server that organizes and streams a user's own movie, TV, music, and photo library to almost any device, offering a free core server plus an optional Emby Premiere subscription for advanced features.
enrichment.seo_title: Emby Review 2026: Media Server Features & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Emby reviewed for 2026: free media server features, Emby Premiere pricing, live TV and DVR, and how it compares to Plex and Jellyfin.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Emby is a personal media server application that lets users organize, manage, and stream their own collection of movies, TV shows, music, and photos to virtually any device, from smart TVs and streaming boxes to phones, game consoles, and web browsers. Its origins trace back to a project called Media Browser, an open-source plugin for Windows Media Center created by developer Luke Pulverenti to provide a friendlier interface for accessing a personal digital media collection. As interest in dedicated home media servers grew and Windows Media Center itself was discontinued by Microsoft, the project evolved beyond its original plugin format, and in 2015 it was rebranded as Emby, becoming a standalone, full-featured media server rather than an add-on to another product.

A defining and sometimes contentious moment in Emby's history came in 2018, when the development team moved the project from an open-source model to a proprietary, closed-source one. Wikipedia notes that as of Emby version 3.5.3, the software 'has been relicensed and is now closed-source, while open source components will be moved to plugins,' and that the proprietary builds distributed through Emby's own website cannot be rebuilt from source because the build scripts themselves are also proprietary. This licensing change was controversial within the self-hosted media community and directly led to the creation of Jellyfin, a free and open-source fork of Emby that continues to be actively maintained as a fully open alternative. Emby itself remains free to download and use for its core functionality today, but the underlying codebase and official builds are no longer open source in the way they were prior to 2018.

Technically, an Emby deployment consists of a server component, typically installed on a home computer, NAS device, or dedicated server running Windows, macOS, Linux, FreeBSD, or various NAS operating systems, which indexes a user's media library, pulls in metadata and artwork, and transcodes video on the fly when needed for the destination device or network. Client apps are available across an unusually wide range of platforms, including Apple TV, iOS, Android, Roku, Amazon Fire TV, Chromecast, PlayStation, Xbox, Samsung and LG smart TVs, and any modern web browser, letting a single media library reach nearly every screen a household owns.

Emby's core server and playback functionality are free to use indefinitely. The paid tier, Emby Premiere, unlocks a set of advanced features layered on top of the free base: Live TV and DVR functionality for recording and streaming over-the-air or IPTV broadcasts, hardware-accelerated transcoding for smoother playback on lower-powered client devices, mobile sync for offline downloads, cloud sync and backup, smart home integrations, and access to premium client apps and skins on certain platforms. As of 2026, Emby Premiere is priced at $4.99 per month, $54 per year, or a $119 one-time lifetime purchase, giving users flexibility between a low-commitment monthly subscription and a single upfront payment for permanent access.

Emby's target users are home media enthusiasts who want to consolidate their personal movie, TV, music, and photo collections into a single, professionally organized, and widely accessible streaming interface, effectively building a private, self-hosted alternative to commercial streaming services using content they already own. Its most direct competitors are Plex, the dominant player in the personal media server space with a broadly similar freemium model and Plex Pass subscription, and Jellyfin, the fully open-source fork born directly out of Emby's 2018 licensing change, which appeals strongly to users who specifically want to avoid any proprietary software or account requirements. Emby differentiates itself with a long-standing focus on Live TV/DVR functionality, broad hardware and NAS compatibility, and a flexible lifetime-purchase pricing option that neither Plex nor Jellyfin offers in quite the same form, though its move away from a fully open-source model in 2018 remains a point some self-hosting purists weigh against it when choosing between Emby, Plex, and Jellyfin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Emby is a personal media server that organizes a user's own movies, TV shows, music, and photos and streams them to nearly any device, evolving out of the open-source Media Browser project for Windows Media Center before rebranding as Emby in 2015.

In 2018 Emby moved from an open-source model to a closed-source, proprietary one, a change that directly led to the creation of the fully open-source Jellyfin fork; Emby's core server remains free to use today even though its codebase is no longer fully open source.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Emby server indexes and organizes a personal media library with metadata and artwork, and transcodes video on the fly for playback across an unusually wide range of client platforms, including smart TVs, game consoles, and mobile devices.
Emby Premiere, the paid tier, adds Live TV and DVR recording, hardware-accelerated transcoding, offline mobile sync, cloud backup, and smart home integrations on top of the free base experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Emby's core server and streaming functionality are free to use indefinitely, with no time limit or forced trial expiration.
Emby Premiere is available for $4.99/month, $54/year, or a $119 one-time lifetime purchase, unlocking Live TV/DVR, hardware transcoding, and other advanced features.

features.description: Native apps span Apple TV, iOS, Android, Roku, Fire TV, Chromecast, PlayStation, Xbox, smart TVs, and web browsers.

features.title: On-the-fly transcoding
features.description: Converts video in real time to match the format and bandwidth needs of the destination device or network.

features.title: Live TV and DVR (Premiere)
features.description: Records and streams over-the-air or IPTV live television content, available with an Emby Premiere subscription.

features.title: Hardware-accelerated transcoding (Premiere)
features.description: Uses GPU hardware acceleration for smoother, more efficient transcoding on Premiere subscriptions.

features.title: Offline mobile sync (Premiere)
features.description: Downloads media to mobile devices for offline playback without an active server connection.

features.title: Cross-platform server deployment
features.description: Server software runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, FreeBSD, and many NAS devices.

features.title: Cloud sync and smart home integration (Premiere)
features.description: Backs up server configuration to the cloud and connects with smart home platforms for Premiere subscribers.

pros: Core server and streaming functionality are free to use indefinitely with no forced trial expiration
Extremely wide client app support across smart TVs, consoles, mobile, and web
Flexible Premiere pricing, including a one-time lifetime purchase option not offered by most competitors
Long-standing, mature feature set including Live TV/DVR support
cons: Moved from open-source to closed-source licensing in 2018, a point of concern for self-hosting purists
Advanced features like Live TV/DVR and hardware transcoding require a paid Premiere subscription
Smaller active development community than its fully open-source fork, Jellyfin
Requires self-hosting on owned hardware or a NAS, unlike commercial streaming services

faqs.question: Is Emby free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Emby's core media server and streaming functionality are free to use indefinitely. An optional Emby Premiere subscription unlocks advanced features like Live TV, DVR, and hardware transcoding.

faqs.question: How much does Emby Premiere cost?
faqs.answer: Emby Premiere is priced at $4.99/month, $54/year, or a $119 one-time lifetime purchase.

faqs.question: Is Emby open source?
faqs.answer: Emby began as the open-source Media Browser project, but in 2018 it moved to a closed-source, proprietary model. This change led to the creation of Jellyfin, a fully open-source fork of Emby.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Emby and Jellyfin?
faqs.answer: Jellyfin is a free, fully open-source fork of Emby created after Emby moved to closed-source licensing in 2018. Emby remains free for core use but is closed-source and offers an optional paid Premiere tier, while Jellyfin is entirely open source with no paid tier.

faqs.question: What platforms does Emby support?
faqs.answer: Emby server runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, FreeBSD, and various NAS devices, while client apps are available on Apple TV, iOS, Android, Roku, Fire TV, Chromecast, PlayStation, Xbox, smart TVs, and web browsers.

faqs.question: Who created Emby?
faqs.answer: Emby was created by developer Luke Pulverenti, evolving out of his earlier open-source Media Browser project for Windows Media Center before being rebranded as Emby in 2015.

use_cases.title: Home movie and TV streaming
use_cases.description: Households use Emby to organize and stream their personal movie and TV collections to smart TVs and streaming devices throughout the home.

use_cases.title: Live TV and DVR replacement
use_cases.description: Emby Premiere subscribers use Live TV and DVR functionality to record and stream over-the-air or IPTV broadcasts without a traditional cable box.

enrichment.short_description: Emplifi is an enterprise customer experience platform combining social media marketing, social customer care, and social commerce tools, formed in 2021 from the merger of Socialbakers and Astute Solutions.
enrichment.starting_price: From $1,249/month (Social Marketing Essential); other products are custom-quoted
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Emplifi in 2026: pricing tiers, social marketing, customer care, and commerce features reviewed for this enterprise customer experience platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Emplifi is an enterprise-grade customer experience platform focused on social media marketing, social customer care, and social commerce, used by large brands to manage how they market to, support, and sell to customers across social and digital channels. The company in its current form was created in June 2021 through the rebranding and combination of two previously separate businesses: Astute Solutions, a customer engagement software company originally founded in 1995, and Socialbakers, a well-known social media analytics and marketing platform. Astute Solutions acquired Socialbakers in September 2020, and roughly nine months later the combined organization relaunched under the single Emplifi brand, unifying the marketing-analytics strength of Socialbakers with the customer-care and CRM heritage of Astute into one product suite. Emplifi is headquartered in Columbus, Ohio, and operates internationally with employees across North America, Europe, and Asia; estimates place its current headcount at roughly 540 to 550 employees as of 2026, and the company has raised institutional backing from investors including Sixth Street, LionTree, Golub Capital BDC, and Audax Private Equity, with the business valued at roughly $1 billion.

Emplifi organizes its product suite into three connected pillars. Social Marketing covers social media publishing, scheduling, content calendaring, competitive benchmarking, and analytics across platforms like Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, and X, helping marketing teams plan and measure campaigns at scale. Social Care focuses on customer service delivered through social and messaging channels, giving support teams a unified inbox, automation, and AI-assisted response tools to manage high volumes of customer inquiries across platforms. Social Commerce connects social content and customer interactions to on-site shopping experiences, including ratings and reviews management and tools designed to turn social engagement directly into purchases. Across all three pillars, Emplifi has increasingly positioned itself around what it calls 'Autonomous Customer Experience,' layering AI-driven automation and predictive analytics on top of its existing tools to reduce manual work for large marketing, care, and commerce teams; some of the most advanced, predictive AI capabilities in its Social Care and Social Commerce 'Intelligent' tiers were, as of the most recent product roadmap, described as not yet generally available and slated for later rollout.

Emplifi's pricing is structured around named tiers within each of its three product pillars: Essential, Advanced, and Intelligent. For Social Marketing, published starting prices show Essential from roughly $1,249 per month for teams beginning to unify their social channels, and Advanced from roughly $2,499 per month for mid-sized teams needing deeper collaboration tools, while the top Intelligent tier is available only via a custom quote for large enterprises with complex, multi-brand operations. Social Care and Social Commerce tiers are priced entirely on a custom-quote basis rather than published rates, reflecting the more variable, deployment-specific nature of customer service and commerce integrations. Across all tiers and products, Emplifi states that pricing scales with business outcomes rather than seat count, and that plans include unlimited user licenses and unlimited owned social profile connections, a notable departure from the common industry practice of charging per user seat.

Emplifi's target customers are mid-market and enterprise brands, typically consumer-facing companies with substantial social media presence and customer service volume, that need to coordinate marketing, support, and commerce activities across multiple social and digital channels from a single platform, often across many regional or product-line social accounts simultaneously. Its competitors include Sprinklr and Hootsuite for enterprise social media management, Sprout Social for mid-market social marketing and engagement, and Khoros and Salesforce Social Studio for social customer care specifically. Emplifi differentiates itself by combining marketing, care, and commerce in a single connected suite rather than requiring separate tools for each function, by its unlimited-seat pricing philosophy, and by its ongoing investment in AI-driven, predictive customer-experience automation branded as Autonomous Customer Experience, which industry analyst Gartner recognized by naming Emplifi a Leader in the social customer experience space in 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Emplifi is an enterprise customer experience platform formed in June 2021 from the merger of Socialbakers, a social media analytics company, and Astute Solutions, a customer engagement company founded in 1995.

Headquartered in Columbus, Ohio, with roughly 540-550 employees across North America, Europe, and Asia, Emplifi is backed by investors including Sixth Street and Audax Private Equity at a valuation of around $1 billion.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform is organized into three pillars: Social Marketing for publishing, scheduling, and analytics; Social Care for unified customer service across social and messaging channels; and Social Commerce for connecting social engagement to on-site shopping and reviews.

Emplifi is increasingly positioned around AI-driven 'Autonomous Customer Experience' automation, with some of its most advanced predictive features in the Intelligent tiers still in the process of general rollout.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Emplifi prices each of its three product pillars in Essential, Advanced, and Intelligent tiers. Social Marketing publishes starting prices of roughly $1,249/month for Essential and $2,499/month for Advanced, with Intelligent custom-quoted.

Social Care and Social Commerce tiers are entirely custom-quoted, and Emplifi states that all plans include unlimited user licenses and unlimited owned social profile connections, with pricing scaled to business outcomes rather than seat count.

features.title: Social media publishing and scheduling
features.description: Plan, schedule, and publish content across major social platforms from a single content calendar.

features.title: Social media analytics and benchmarking
features.description: Measure campaign performance and benchmark against competitors using inherited Socialbakers analytics technology.

features.title: Unified social customer care inbox
features.description: Manage customer service inquiries from multiple social and messaging channels in one unified agent inbox.

features.title: AI-assisted customer care automation
features.description: Use AI-driven automation and suggested responses to help support teams handle high volumes of customer inquiries.

features.title: Ratings and reviews management
features.description: Collect, monitor, and respond to customer ratings and reviews as part of the Social Commerce pillar.

features.title: Social commerce integration
features.description: Connect social content and engagement directly to on-site shopping experiences to drive conversions.

features.title: Unlimited user licenses
features.description: All Emplifi plans include unlimited user seats rather than charging per additional team member.

features.title: Unlimited owned profile connections
features.description: Connect an unlimited number of owned brand social profiles across supported platforms on any plan.

pros: Combines social marketing, customer care, and commerce in one connected platform rather than requiring separate tools
Unlimited user licenses and unlimited owned profile connections on all plans, unusual in an industry that typically charges per seat
Deep analytics heritage inherited from Socialbakers, a well-established social analytics brand
Recognized as a Leader by Gartner in the social customer experience space in 2026
cons: Pricing for Social Care and Social Commerce is entirely custom-quoted with nothing published publicly
Published Social Marketing starting prices, from roughly $1,249/month, put it out of reach for small businesses and solo marketers
Some of the most advanced 'Intelligent' AI-driven features are not yet generally available across all product lines
Enterprise-focused positioning means a sales conversation is required to get full pricing for most of the suite

faqs.question: How much does Emplifi cost?
faqs.answer: Emplifi's Social Marketing product publishes starting prices of roughly $1,249/month for the Essential tier and $2,499/month for Advanced, while Social Care, Social Commerce, and the top Intelligent tiers are priced via custom quote.

faqs.question: When was Emplifi founded?
faqs.answer: Emplifi was formed in June 2021 through the rebranding and merger of Socialbakers and Astute Solutions, the latter of which was originally founded in 1995.

faqs.question: Where is Emplifi headquartered?
faqs.answer: Emplifi is headquartered in Columbus, Ohio, United States, with employees across North America, Europe, and Asia.

faqs.question: What products does Emplifi offer?
faqs.answer: Emplifi's suite is organized into three pillars: Social Marketing for publishing and analytics, Social Care for unified customer service, and Social Commerce for connecting social engagement to on-site shopping and reviews.

faqs.question: Does Emplifi charge per user seat?
faqs.answer: No. Emplifi states that all of its plans include unlimited user licenses and unlimited owned social profile connections, with pricing scaled to business outcomes instead of seat count.

faqs.question: Who are Emplifi's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Emplifi competes primarily with Sprinklr, Hootsuite, Sprout Social, and Khoros across enterprise social media marketing and customer care.

use_cases.title: Enterprise social media marketing at scale
use_cases.description: Large consumer brands use Emplifi's Social Marketing tools to plan, publish, and analyze content across many social accounts and regions.

use_cases.title: Unified social customer service
use_cases.description: Customer support organizations use Emplifi's Social Care inbox and AI-assisted automation to manage high volumes of inquiries across social and messaging channels.

use_cases.title: Social-driven commerce and reviews
use_cases.description: Retail and e-commerce brands use Emplifi's Social Commerce tools to manage ratings, reviews, and social-to-shopping conversion.

features: Built for large, complex enterprise operations
Faster workflows for growing support teams
Optimized customer experience tools for growing brands
